### Is this your first time submitting a feature request?

- [x] I have read the [expectations for open source contributors](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/contributing/oss-expectations)
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this feature
- [x] I am requesting a straightforward extension of existing dbt functionality, rather than a Big Idea better suited to a discussion

### Describe the feature

Add two new fields to existing events:
1. `catalogs` (count) in [`track_resource_counts`](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/blob/c9aaa9d27cd6cd544e4e2926fb2c418f0e5cc11a/core/dbt/tracking.py#L281), which fires at the end of parsing
2. `catalog_type` in [`track_model_run`](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/blob/c9aaa9d27cd6cd544e4e2926fb2c418f0e5cc11a/core/dbt/tracking.py#L294), which fires when each model is actually executed
    - For the current `catalogs.yml` spec - `catalog_type: built_in | iceberg_rest | unity | ...`
    - We're about to propose an updated `catalogs` spec (GH discussion forthcoming) that will have `catalog_type: glue | unity | polaris | ...`

This will enable us to answer questions like:
- How many models are configured with a `catalog_name`? Without leaking any identifying info about the names of the catalog, because we're just sending up the `type`
- How many distinct catalogs / catalog types per project?
- Which catalog types are the most popular? In combination with which adapters/DWHs?
- (Indirectly) Track adoption of the old/new spec, because some `catalog_type` values will change between them

### Describe alternatives you've considered

A totally new `catalog_info` event. This might make sense in the future, but it doesn't feel necessary yet.

### Who will this benefit?

Us, the maintainers of dbt, working to make Iceberg integrations easier to adopt

### Are you interested in contributing this feature?

I know that @ash2shukla is
dbt-labs/dbt-adapters#1122.title: [Feature] Add Snowplow Tracking on catalogs.yml

### Current Behavior

When executing a dbt command with `-t` or `--target` command line argument and the previous command was executed without the target argument, dbt is unable to do a partial parse even if the target is the same as defined in profiles.yml.


### Expected Behavior

As long as the target stays the same between between executions, a partial parse should be possible if nothing else has changed.

### Steps To Reproduce

1. Create a dbt project with `dbt init` with any connector.
2. Create a profile.yml with at least one output and set `target` to that output
3. Run `dbt parse` -> does a full parse (expected)
4. Run `dbt parse -t <same value as profiles.yml target>` -> does a full parse again (unexpected)

dbt-labs/dbt-core#7867.body:
### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [X] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [X] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior

With dbt 1.5 the `compile` command was extended to allow "interactive" compilation of an arbitrary node by using syntax similar to :
`dbt compile --select name_of_node`

In our testing we found this seems to work for all types of node, including **snapshots**.

However, the vanilla operation of `dbt compile` does not support snapshots. The [documentation](https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/commands/compile) states "dbt compile generates executable SQL from source `model`, `test`, and `analysis` files." i.e. it does not include snapshots and indeed if `dbt compile` is run for a project no code files are created for snapshots in "/target/compiled/..".

This has created an inconsistency where one usage of `compile` will generate output for a snapshot but another will not. 

This is not a bug as such but I think the inconsistent behaviour is potentially confusing for users, and it's not clear why standard `compile` doesn't already work for snapshots since it is apparently possible based on the interactive version.  

### Expected Behavior

All usages of `dbt compile` should produce compiled code for snapshots i.e. non-interactive uses of `compile` should generate the same compiled code as interactive use.

### Steps To Reproduce

1. Create a snapshot file
2. Run `dbt compile`
3. Note no output produced under "/target/compiled/"
4. Run `dbt compile --select <name_of_snapshot>`
5. Note compiled code output to command line

### Relevant log output

_No response_

### Environment

```markdown
- OS: Windows
- Python: 3.10.10
- dbt: 1.5.0
```


### Which database adapter are you using with dbt?

bigquery

### Additional Context

_No response_
dbt-labs/dbt-core#7867.title: [CT-2699] [Feature] "interactive" compile should include the compiled code of a snapshot

dbt-labs/dbt-core#8248.body:
### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [X] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [X] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior

Suppose you have a model (`order.sql`) setup in a group (`example`) with access set to private. If you execute a macro that `ref`s this private model, dbt will abort with an `AttributeError: 'Macro' object has no attribute 'group'` exception.

```console
19:22:02  Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/redacted/dbt_project/venv/lib/python3.9/site-packages/dbt/adapters/snowflake/connections.py", line 255, in exception_handler
    yield
  File "/redacted/dbt_project/venv/lib/python3.9/site-packages/dbt/adapters/base/impl.py", line 1043, in execute_macro
    result = macro_function(**kwargs)
  File "/redacted/dbt_project/venv/lib/python3.9/site-packages/dbt/clients/jinja.py", line 330, in __call__
    return self.call_macro(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/redacted/dbt_project/venv/lib/python3.9/site-packages/dbt/clients/jinja.py", line 257, in call_macro
    return macro(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/redacted/dbt_project/venv/lib/python3.9/site-packages/jinja2/runtime.py", line 763, in __call__
    return self._invoke(arguments, autoescape)
  File "/redacted/dbt_project/venv/lib/python3.9/site-packages/jinja2/runtime.py", line 777, in _invoke
    rv = self._func(*arguments)
  File "<template>", line 35, in macro
  File "/redacted/dbt_project/venv/lib/python3.9/site-packages/jinja2/sandbox.py", line 393, in call
    return __context.call(__obj, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/redacted/dbt_project/venv/lib/python3.9/site-packages/jinja2/runtime.py", line 298, in call
    return __obj(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/redacted/dbt_project/venv/lib/python3.9/site-packages/dbt/context/providers.py", line 259, in __call__
    return self.resolve(name, package, version)
  File "/redacted/dbt_project/venv/lib/python3.9/site-packages/dbt/context/providers.py", line 508, in resolve
    if not self.model.group or self.model.group != target_model.group:
AttributeError: 'Macro' object has no attribute 'group'
```

This issue seems to originate in the `resolve` method in `RuntimeRefResolver`. Specifically, this chunk of logic:

```python
        elif (
            target_model.resource_type == NodeType.Model
            and target_model.access == AccessType.Private
            # don't raise this reference error for ad hoc 'preview' queries
            and self.model.resource_type != NodeType.SqlOperation
            and self.model.resource_type != NodeType.RPCCall  # TODO: rm
        ):
            if not self.model.group or self.model.group != target_model.group:
                raise DbtReferenceError(
                    unique_id=self.model.unique_id,
                    ref_unique_id=target_model.unique_id,
                    group=cast_to_str(target_model.group),
                )
```
When running a macro via run-operation, `target_model` is not a `ParsedNode`, but rather a `Macro`, which do not have a concept of a `group`. Hence, the `AttributeError`.

### Expected Behavior

Given that macros are not part of the group and access abstraction, I would expect macros executed within a `run-operation` context to be able to ref any model in a dbt project without verifying group membership.

### Steps To Reproduce

1. Create a model, and configure it to be in a group.
2. Create a macro that ref's the grouped model.
3. Attempt to execute the macro via `run-operation`.

### Relevant log output

```shell
See above in current behavior
```


### Environment

```markdown
- OS: MacOS 13.5
- Python: 3.9.6
- dbt: 1.5.4
```


### Which database adapter are you using with dbt?

snowflake

### Additional Context

_No response_
dbt-labs/dbt-core#8248.title: [CT-2889] Macros executed via run-operation cannot ref `private` models

dbt-labs/dbt-core#8278.body:
### Is this your first time submitting a feature request?

- [X] I have read the [expectations for open source contributors](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/contributing/oss-expectations)
- [X] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this feature
- [X] I am requesting a straightforward extension of existing dbt functionality, rather than a Big Idea better suited to a discussion

### Describe the feature

I have code which executes `dbt` and parses `run_results.json` to get the results of the invocation. For other operations, the `message` property contains error messages if the invocation fails. This is not the case for the new run_results artifact written by `run-operation`: https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/blob/6130a6e1d0d29b257fbcd1b17fcd730383d73ce0/core/dbt/task/run_operation.py#L89C3-L89C3

I would like the exception message to be written to the `message` field when the `run-operation` invocation fails. So basically something like `message=None if success else string_containing_exception_from_earlier,`.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Invoking `dbt` with `--log-format json --log-level error` and parsing the results can be a workaround for this limitation. Since `run_results.json` has more useful fields this would mean parsing both to get the required data from the `dbt` invocation.

### Who will this benefit?

Code which parses the `run_results.json` file will have the error message from `run-operation` invocations. So anyone orchestrating dbt in a data pipeline I suppose.

### Are you interested in contributing this feature?

Yes. I'm not that experienced with python, but this feature seems simple enough.

### Anything else?

_No response_
dbt-labs/dbt-core#8278.title: [CT-2907] [Feature] Exception messages in `run_results.json` from `run-operation` invocations

dbt-labs/dbt-core#10992.body:
### Is this your first time submitting a feature request?

- [X] I have read the [expectations for open source contributors](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/contributing/oss-expectations)
- [X] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this feature
- [X] I am requesting a straightforward extension of existing dbt functionality, rather than a Big Idea better suited to a discussion

### Describe the feature

Introduce a new [selector method](https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/methods) in order to explicitly select a selector on the command line, and be able to combine the result with the full power of CLI selection (union, intersection, exclude, etc.).

This would solve #5009, #10991, and #10596 (and probably others). 

[A `selector` method already exists](https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/node-selection/yaml-selectors#selector-inheritance) (see also [#1628](https://github.com/dbt-labs/docs.getdbt.com/issues/1628), #4821, and #4827), but only for selector inheritance within YAML selectors. Unfortunately, this method is not available on the command line (which is what this feature would provide).

#### YAML selectors as subsets
But the real power in this would be to turn YAML selectors into predefined selection subsets (rather than being the primary selection). For example, instead of: `dbt ls -s something,tag:one,tag:two,tag:three,etc.`, we could define a `tag_one_two_three_etc` selector, and then simply do `dbt ls -s something,selector:tag_one_two_three_etc`. This would provide tremendous reusability for cases that do not necessarily deserve new selectors to be created, and would offer the possibility to encapsulate repetitive or complex selection logic in predefined & reusable "selection components".

#### Composition over inheritance
Composition over inheritance is a very valuable software design pattern/approach, and dbt users would gain from it being applied to selectors. Right now, the command line offers composition but no inheritance, whereas YAML selectors offer inheritance but "no composition". YAML composition _is_ possible, _**but at the cost of preemptively creating a multitude of selectors for every possible combination**_, _which very quickly become unmanageable_ (so basically it is multiple inheritance, _rather than composition_, _**and that is exactly where composition over inheritance shines/comes to the rescue**_). So you could imagine: `selector_1_with_abc`, `selector_1_with_def`, `selector_1_with_abc_and_def`, `selector_2_with_abc`, and on and on... and before you know it, _**YAML selectors are no longer DRY**_.

I think this is a serious question/consideration: is selection in dbt meant to be composed, or inherited? Inheritance usually works better for really simple software classes... but in the end, composition almost always offers a superior alternative. An obvious example is that everyone would favor `List(<Animal>)` over `ListOfAnimals` (which would inherit from `List`). Selector inheritance really doesn't seem like the right direction.

#### Grouping & Negating
Another powerful use case is to use YAML selectors to create negative selections, which could then be used in `--exclude selector:not_something`. There is an example described [here](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/10596#issuecomment-2472268215).

#### Dynamic selection
This would also be really useful to better handle cases where the CLI selection is generated dynamically (possibly outside of dbt entirely) in order to produce a much DRYer selection syntax. For example, existing YAML selectors could be offered as parameters to choose from in the selection (with the possibility to compose a selection from them).

To me it seems to be a no-brainer at this point that we must be able to dynamically compose selectors together with other selection criteria (including other selectors). That's how model selection started, and it was the right direction. YAML selectors helped with the ability to define selectors as version-controlled code, _but at the cost of losing the ability to dynamically compose selections_.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

There are none. Right now, we are forced to choose between YAML selectors or CLI selectors, _but we cannot combine both_.

### Who will this benefit?

Dbt users with advanced selection needs. Especially those who want to tweak selectors for custom or ad-hoc use cases.

It would also benefit every DBT user by enabling YAML selectors to be used as subsets on the CLI.

### Are you interested in contributing this feature?

Maybe. Not quite sure where to start.

### Anything else?

_No response_
dbt-labs/dbt-core#10992.title: [Feature] add `selector:` method to combine YAML selectors with command line selection

dbt-labs/dbt-core#11144.body:
### Short description

From https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/2955

"I want to define project-wide variables outside of my dbt_project.yml."
"I want to be able to reference variables in my dbt_project.yml at parse-time."

### Acceptance criteria

- I can define project-wide ("global variables") variables in a `vars.yml` file, separate from my `dbt_project.yml`
```yml
# vars.yml

vars:
  start_date: '2016-06-01'
```
- dbt parses `vars.yml` _before_ parsing `dbt_project.yml`, meaning default values of vars called in `dbt_project.yml` would work the way folks expect, rather than [how it is today](https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/dbt-jinja-functions/dbt-project-yml-context/) (equivalent to how this works when defining vars through the CLI a la `--vars`)

**Option 1 - behavior change**
- You cannot define variables in both places, you must chose the "new" or "legacy" way
- As such, this would need to be behind a behavior change flag
- Open question: Given that behavior change flags are set _in_ `dbt_project.yml`, would this even be possible?

**Option 2 - backwards compatible**
- You can define variables in either places
- Open question: Would we still get the parsing benefit if we went this route?

Current workaround: Have a really massive `dbt_project.yml` file with no ability to reference variables in your project configurations
dbt-labs/dbt-core#11144.title: [implementation] define project-wide vars in vars.yml, outside of dbt_project.yml

### Steps To Reproduce

        materialized='incremental',
        incremental_strategy='microbatch',
        event_time='_event_date',
        begin='2024-12-01',

        batch_size='day'

}}

### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [x] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior

Today, it's possible to configure `enabled` for an individual analysis node:
- within the SQL file: `{{ config(enabled = false) }}`
- in yaml properties for that specific analysis
```yaml
analyses:
  - name: my_analysis
    config:
      enabled: false
```

But it's not possible to configure `analyses` in `dbt_project.yml`:
```yaml
# dbt_project.yml
analyses:
  +enabled: false
```

The `analyses` key is accepted (no parsing error), but the configuration does not seem to apply.

### Expected Behavior

Configuring `enabled` for `analyses` in `dbt_project.yml` should apply that configuration to analyses defined in the project, the same as for any other resource type in dbt.

### Steps To Reproduce

1. Define an analysis: `analyses/my_analysis.sql`
2. `dbt compile -s my_analysis.sql` - it's enabled
3. Disable that analysis via in-file `{{ config() }}` or individual properties in a yaml file 
4. `dbt compile -s my_analysis.sql` - it's disabled
5. Remove those resource-specific configurations, and instead try disabling the analysis in `dbt_project.yml`
6. `dbt compile -s my_analysis.sql` - it's enabled again, showing that the project-level config is not applied

- Python: 3.11.9

dbt-labs/dbt-core#11975.body:
### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [x] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior

When a source shares the sanme identifier with a model name but they are in different schemas (can be in the same database or in different one), when trying a unit test in incremental mode and mocking {{ this }} and the source, the dbt unit  test would create two CTEs with the same name which causes the SQL to not compile as the source and model would have different schema.

select * from (
        
  WITH  __dbt__cte__clients as (
   -- the schema of the source
  )
  , __dbt__cte__clients as (
    -- the schema of the model
  )
  SELECT 
     ...
  FROM __dbt__cte__clients
  WHERE modified_datetime >= (SELECT max(modified_datetime) FROM __dbt__cte__clients)

) as __dbt_sbq
where false
limit 0

### Expected Behavior

I would expect that the naming of the CTEs in the test is not subject only of the identifier as it is a common practice that a staging table can have a raw table with the same name in a different database/schema; I would hope that the unit test SQL would be form in the following way:

select * from (
        
  WITH  __dbt__cte__my_raw_source_raw_clients as (
   -- the schema of the source
  )
  , __dbt__cte__clients as (
    -- the schema of the model
  )
  SELECT 
     ...
  FROM __dbt__cte__my_raw_source_raw_clients
  WHERE modified_datetime >= (SELECT max(modified_datetime) FROM __dbt__cte__clients)

) as __dbt_sbq
where false
limit 0


As it can be seen, for the naming of the sources I would just expect the CTE naming to contain the source name to avoid CTE naming crashes with models, anything that could separate the CTE source name with the CTE model name could be fine.

### Steps To Reproduce

Set up an example as following:

sources.yml

sources:
  - name: my_raw_source
    database: DEV_RAW
    schema: ANYTING
    tables:
      - name: raw_clients
        identifier: clients


core_models.yml

models:
  - name: clients
    config:
      database: DEV_STAGING
      schema: ANYTING
      materialized: incremental
      unique_key: ["col_1", "col_2"]


clients.sql

SELECT source.any_variant_column:data.any_attribute as col_1
FROM {{ source('my_raw_source,', 'raw_clients') }} as source
WHERE modfied_datetime >= (SELECT max(modfied_datetime_ FROM {{ this }})

unit_test.yml

unit_tests:
  - name: test_clients_incremental
    model: payroll_events
    description: Test that checks if payroll_events logic is correct for incremental mode
    overrides:
      macros:
        is_incremental: true
    given:
      - input: this
        format: sql
        fixture: a_fixture_for_this
      - input: source('my_raw_source','raw_clients')
        format: sql
        fixture: a_fixture_for_the_source
    expect:
      format: sql
      fixture: result_fixture


when running `dbt test -s test_clients_incremental` the test will fail as dbt would create two ctes with the same name for {{ this }} and for the source

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Environment

```markdown
- OS: MacOS
- Python: 3.11
- dbt: >= 1.9.*
```

### Which database adapter are you using with dbt?

snowflake

### Additional Context

_No response_

dbt-labs/dbt-core#12006.body:
### Please describe the issue and your proposals.

We've experienced user-facing performance regressions in their jobs when introducing schema changes across dbt versions in the past:
* https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/11853
* https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/11732

From spiking the root causes of these issues, untroducing false positives in state:modified shouldn't be as pervasive as I had originally thought. So, not for _every_ minor evolution of our manifest artifact contract.
The edge case we stumbled on throughout August was that we were introducing minor schema evolutions in such a way where the default value of a new config was implicitly set. For example, propagating existing top-level meta/tag values to config.meta/config.tag meant that previous manifests with implicit null values for config.meta/config.tag were being compared to real values if the resource already had top-level meta/tags defined.

If we had introduced upgrade/migration logic for previous manifests (could check the recorded versions), this issue of false positives could have been avoided entirely.

**On testing:**
Unfortunately setting up a bulletproof regression test would require an exhaustive test project that has every possible property/config field represented. It'd be hard to maintain and keep up to date. We could use our 'happy path project' as a smoke test at the very least though.

Additionally, I'd suggest we go with a catch-all check in CI that simply asserts that a new upgrader was introduced anytime an artifact schema change is made (with an opt-out available if it is unnecessary). We do something similar to ensure schema evolutions are in fact minor evolutions already! And in this case we can still have a reasonable baseline test that can get updated as we introduce changes.
dbt-labs/dbt-core#12006.title: Introduce testing + upgrade framework to avoid state:modified false positives as a result of schema changes

### Housekeeping

- [x] I am a maintainer of dbt-core

### Short description

After https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/12043

Some python UDFs may want to use 3rd party PyPi packages - snowflake [docs](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/udf/python/udf-python-packages)

The `package` would need to be specified during the create function statement:
```
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION sklearn_udf()
  RETURNS FLOAT
  LANGUAGE PYTHON
  RUNTIME_VERSION = 3.9
  ARTIFACT_REPOSITORY = snowflake.snowpark.pypi_shared_repository
  PACKAGES = ('scikit-learn')
  HANDLER = 'udf'
  AS
$$
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

def udf():
  X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
  X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.25, random_state=42)

  model = RandomForestClassifier()
  model.fit(X_train, y_train)
  return model.score(X_test, y_test)
$$;
```

### Acceptance criteria

- I can specify which `packages` I want to use in my python UDF

### Context

- See [internal slack thread](https://dbt-labs.slack.com/archives/CSSHB76QL/p1758142720330089?thread_ts=1758030491.041499&cid=CSSHB76QL)
- Bigquery [docs](https://docs.cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/user-defined-functions-python#install_a_package_from_the_python_package_index)

### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [x] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior

The Fusion engine uses any custom `ref` overrides within generic data tests and unit tests, but the Core engine raises errors.

### Expected Behavior

Expect parity between Fusion and Core. In this case, for Core to also utilize custom overrides of `ref` in generic data tests and unit tests.

### Steps To Reproduce

I know the following example is a bit complicated. If someone has a more minimal example, please share all the file details! 🙏 

Create these files:

`macros/ref.sql`

Which adds a `revision` argument (to be another alias for `version` / `v`).

```sql
{% macro ref() %}

-- extract user-provided positional and keyword arguments
{% set version = 2 %}
{% set version = kwargs.get('revision') or kwargs.get('version') or kwargs.get('v') %}
{% set packagename = none %}
{%- if (varargs | length) == 1 -%}
    {% set modelname = varargs[0] %}
{%- else -%}
    {% set packagename = varargs[0] %}
    {% set modelname = varargs[1] %}
{% endif %}

-- call builtins.ref based on provided positional arguments
{% set rel = None %}
{% if packagename is not none %}
    {% set rel = builtins.ref(packagename, modelname, version=version) %}
{% else %}
    {% set rel = builtins.ref(modelname, version=version) %}
{% endif %}

{% do return(rel) %}

{% endmacro %}
```

select 1 as customer_id
```

select 2 as customer_id

dbt-labs/dbt-core#12329.body:
### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [x] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior


When running `dbt compile` or `dbt run` against sufficiently large or complex dbt models, compilation fails with an error originating from `sqlparse` indicating that the maximum grouping token count or grouping depth has been exceeded (e.g., exceeding `MAX_GROUPING_TOKEN` or `MAX_GROUPING_DEPTH`).

This occurs even though:
- The SQL being compiled is valid
- The SQL originates from trusted dbt project code
- The failure is not related to runtime execution in the warehouse

The current mitigation in dbt-core is to upper-bound the `sqlparse` dependency version to 0.5.4, which avoids the error but prevents use of newer `sqlparse` releases.


### Expected Behavior


`dbt compile` and `dbt run` should successfully compile large or complex dbt models without failing due to `sqlparse` grouping limits, provided the SQL is valid.

Because dbt-core operates on trusted SQL generated from project code, grouping limits intended to protect against untrusted input should be explicitly configured or relaxed, rather than causing compilation failures or requiring dependency pinning.


### Steps To Reproduce

1. Use a dbt-core version that allows newer `sqlparse` versions (or remove the upper bound).
2. Create or use an existing dbt model with:
   - Large CTE chains, or
   - Deeply nested expressions, or
   - Repeated templated SQL expansion
3. Run:
   ```bash
   dbt compile

### Relevant log output

```shell
This log is from the original error:

[2026-01-06, 17:30:31 UTC] {subprocess.py:106} INFO - 17:30:31  614 of 625 START sql table model sample_schema.sample_table .............. [RUN]
[2026-01-06, 17:30:34 UTC] {subprocess.py:106} INFO - 17:30:34  Unhandled error while executing 
[2026-01-06, 17:30:34 UTC] {subprocess.py:106} INFO - Maximum number of tokens exceeded (10000).
[2026-01-06, 17:30:34 UTC] {subprocess.py:106} INFO - 17:30:34  614 of 625 ERROR creating sql table model sample_schema.sample_table ..... [ERROR in 1.06s]
```

### Environment

```markdown
- OS: Linux 5.15.0-1101-azure x86_64
- Python: 3.12.3
- dbt-core: 1.10.18
```

### Which database adapter are you using with dbt?

snowflake

### Additional Context

- The behavior in sqlparse is intentional and reflects a security-oriented change to protect against denial-of-service scenarios when parsing untrusted SQL.
- According to the sqlparse maintainer, downstream consumers that operate on trusted SQL should explicitly configure MAX_GROUPING_TOKEN and/or MAX_GROUPING_DEPTH, or set them to None where appropriate.
- dbt-core currently does not override these values, despite compiling trusted SQL, leading to failures for legitimate dbt models.
- The existing fix in #12303 upper-bounds the sqlparse version to 0.5.4, which is a temporary workaround and does not address the underlying incompatibility as 0.5.4 also has the same token tracking, but simply returns None instead of raising an error.
- A permanent fix would involve explicitly configuring sqlparse grouping limits within dbt-core rather than pinning dependency versions.

The comments from the maintainer of sqlparse can be found here:
https://github.com/andialbrecht/sqlparse/discussions/829

> Thanks for bringing this up! It shows me, that the recent change in 0.5.5 (issue https://github.com/andialbrecht/sqlparse/issues/827) was correct.
> 
> 0.5.4 introduced this token and recursion tracking to avoid DoS attacks. Unfortunately instead of raising an exception it returned None when one of the two security guards were triggered. 0.5.5 changed this to raise an exception (what you see now).
> 
> The reason is a security-first approach: If you have a valid use-case where you know the statements that are processed by sqlparse it's perfectly fine to disable these constraints (the old behavior before 0.5.4). But if you "just use" sqlparse these constraints cover you from DoS attacks.
> 
> The change is documented here: https://sqlparse.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#security-and-performance-considerations
> 
> TL;DR:
> 
> import sqlparse.engine.grouping
> 
> # Increase limits (use with caution)
> sqlparse.engine.grouping.MAX_GROUPING_DEPTH = 200
> sqlparse.engine.grouping.MAX_GROUPING_TOKENS = 50000
> 
> # Disable limits completely (use with extreme caution)
> sqlparse.engine.grouping.MAX_GROUPING_DEPTH = None
> sqlparse.engine.grouping.MAX_GROUPING_TOKENS = None
dbt-labs/dbt-core#12329.title: [Bug] Permanent fix needed for sqlparse grouping limits (follow-up to #12303)

dbt-labs/dbt-core#12342.body:
### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [x] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior

When defining a Python UDF with the documented `entry_point` and `runtime_version` config, dbt emits a `CustomKeyInConfigDeprecation` warning suggesting the key should be moved to `config.meta`. However, `entry_point` is a documented **required** configuration for Python UDFs per the [official documentation](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/udfs#defining-udfs-in-dbt), and moving them to `config.meta` results in errors.

### Expected Behavior

No warning should be emitted since `entry_point` and `runtime_version` is a documented required config for Python UDFs.

### Steps To Reproduce

1. In dbt core v1.11.2 Create a python UDF:

**`functions/clean_html.py`**
```
def main(html_string: str) -> str:
    return html_string  # simplified example
```

**`functions/_functions.yml`**
```
version: 2

functions:
  - name: clean_html
    description: Cleans HTML from a string
    config:
      runtime_version: "3.11" # <-- triggers the warning
      entry_point: main  # <-- triggers the warning
    arguments:
      - name: html_string
        data_type: string
    returns:
      data_type: string
```

2. run any dbt command

### Relevant log output

```shell
dbt compile --show-all-deprecations --no-partial-parse
18:05:59  Running with dbt=1.11.2
18:05:59  Registered adapter: snowflake=1.11.1
18:05:59  [WARNING][CustomKeyInConfigDeprecation]: Deprecated functionality
Custom key `entry_point` found in `config` at path `functions[0].config` in file
`functions/_functions.yml`. Custom config keys should move into the
`config.meta`.
18:05:59  [WARNING][CustomKeyInConfigDeprecation]: Deprecated functionality
Custom key `runtime_version` found in `config` at path `functions[0].config` in
file `functions/_functions.yml`. Custom config keys should move into the
`config.meta`.
18:06:02  Found 173 models, 2 seeds, 1060 data tests, 94 sources, 987 macros
18:06:02  
18:06:02  Concurrency: 4 threads (target='dev')
18:06:02  
18:06:09  [WARNING][DeprecationsSummary]: Deprecated functionality
Summary of encountered deprecations:
- CustomKeyInConfigDeprecation: 2 occurrences
```

### Environment

```markdown
- OS: Mac OS 15.7.3
- Python: 3.11.9
- dbt: dbt-core 1.11.2
```

### Which database adapter are you using with dbt?

snowflake

### Additional Context

The [UDF documentation](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/build/udfs) explicitly states:
> The following configs are required when defining a Python UDF:
> - runtime_version — Specify the Python version to run.
> - entry_point — Specify the Python function to be called.

The warning creates noise in CI/CD pipelines and production logs, and the suggested remediation (moving to config.meta) breaks the UDF functionality.
dbt-labs/dbt-core#12342.title: [Bug] `CustomKeyInConfigDeprecation` warning incorrectly triggered for documented Python UDF config

### Is this your first time submitting a feature request?

- [x] I have read the [expectations for open source contributors](https://docs.getdbt.com/docs/contributing/oss-expectations)
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this feature
- [x] I am requesting a straightforward extension of existing dbt functionality, rather than a Big Idea better suited to a discussion

### Describe the feature

Currently when dbt-core calculates a checksum, only whitespaces are removed from the beginning and end of the file contents. Rather than doing a strip only, we propose removing all whitespaces from the file contents and converting the resulting string to lowercase before calculating the checksum. This will have the effect of making checksums to be the same for semantically similar data, rather than brittle with respect to whitespace and case changes.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Who will this benefit?

This will benefit users that use a mantle/core previous state manifest in dbt-fusion.

        my_column_b: varchar(32)
```

You don't have to specify column types, if you don't, we try to infer what type the column is. Sometimes this inference doesn't think it's the type you know it is, which is why we allow for specifying the type.

A problem arises though when you specify a column type for a column that _doesn't exist_. In our example above let's say that `my_column_b` doesn't exist. Well when we dbt goes to determine column types a warning will emitted from `agate` (which we use for inferring/setting types) like the following

> RuntimeWarning: "my_column_b" does not match the name of any column in this table.

The warning doesn't tell you _which_ seed has the problem. If you only have a few seeds and none have a `my_column_b` solving the problem is quite trivial. Run a search on your YAML files for seeds + your dbt_project.yaml and remove any references to `my_column_b`. But what if you have a lot of seed and some or most have a `my_column_b`? **Well then it becomes a very annoying problem.** To resolve it you need to find every seed with a config setting for `my_column_b` _and then cross-reference it with the seeds themselves_ 🫠 

Instead of letting `agate` raise the warning, we should first check the columns we are handing to agate, and the column names we're handing to agate to force the type of. If a column name with a type specification doesn't exist in the columns of the seed, we (`dbt-core`) should raise a warning with better information about the seed, and then also not hand that column type specification to agate.



### Acceptance criteria

* we raise a warning with seed information when a column type is specified for a column that doesn't exist in a seed
* agate doesn't raise a warning about the same column

### Suggested Tests

1. Test that we get our warning
2. Test that we don't get the agate warning

### Impact to Other Teams

NA

### Will backports be required?

No

### Context

_No response_

dbt-labs/dbt-core#12455.body:
### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [x] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior

This is a bug in the same vein as https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/11885 - however it is slightly different in that the `ref('other_table')` is always resolving to the deferred relation, even IF the `other_table` is built as part of the selection - aka

```sql
-- bar is built in CI schema.
create or replace db.ci.bar as (...)

-- foo's turn to be built in CI schema.
create or replace db.ci.foo (
  fk int references db.prod.bar (id)
) as (
  select * from db.ci.bar
)
```

^ Observe that:
* The model body `ref('bar')` resolves correctly to `db.ci.bar`
* The model foreign key `ref('bar')` resolves incorrectly to `db.prod.bar`

### Expected Behavior

Expectation:

```sql
-- bar is built in CI schema.
create or replace db.ci.bar as (...)

-- foo's turn to be built in CI schema.
create or replace db.ci.foo (
  fk int references db.ci.bar (id) -- this should also resolve to the ci schema's version of bar.
) as (
  select * from db.ci.bar
)
```

### Steps To Reproduce

We will setup 2 profiles so we can test this.

```yaml
# profiles.yml
sf:
  target: ci
  outputs:
    ci:
      type: snowflake
      schema: ci
      ...
    prod:
      type: snowflake
      schema: prod
      ...
```

And our dbt project:

```yaml
# dbt_project.yml
name: analytics
profile: sf
version: "1.0.0"

models:
  analytics:
    +materialized: table

# models/schema.yml
models:
  - name: foo
    config:
      materialized: table
      contract: { enforced: true }
    columns:
      - name: pk
        data_type: int
        constraints:
          - type: primary_key
      - name: fk
        data_type: int
        constraints:
          - type: foreign_key
            to: ref('bar')
            to_columns:
              - id
  - name: bar
    config:
      materialized: table
      contract: { enforced: true }
    columns:
      - name: id
        data_type: int
        constraints:
          - type: primary_key
```

```sql
-- models/bar.sql
select 1 id

-- models/foo.sql
select 1 pk, id as fk from {{ ref('bar') }}
```

Lets do a production build into the production schema `prod` (also moving the target folder to do defer)

```sh
$ dbt --debug run -t prod && mv target target_old
...
03:42:28  On model.analytics.bar: create or replace transient table db.prod.bar 
  (
    id int primary key
    )
    as (
    select id
    from (
        select 1 id
    ) as model_subq
    )

03:42:29  On model.analytics.foo: create or replace transient table db.prod.foo
  (
    pk int primary key,
    fk int references db.prod.bar (id)
    )
    as (
    select pk, fk
    from (
        select 1 pk, id as fk from db.prod.bar
    ) as model_subq
    )
```

No issues here.
1. `bar` built as `db.prod.bar`.
2. `foo` selects from `db.prod.bar` in both it's `fk int references ...` and the body `select ... from`.

Let's modify `foo.sql`:

```sql
-- models/foo.sql
select 2 pk, id as fk from {{ ref('bar') }}
```

Now, we do a "CI run" with deferral:

```sh
$ dbt --debug run -s +state:modified+ --defer --state target_old -t ci
...
03:46:34  On model.analytics.bar: create or replace transient table db.ci.bar  
  (
    id int primary key
    )
    as (
    select id
    from (
        select 1 id
    ) as model_subq
    )
...
03:46:35  On model.analytics.foo: create or replace transient table db.ci.foo
  (
    pk int primary key,
    fk int references db.prod.bar (id)
    )
    as (
    select pk, fk
    from (
        select 2 pk, id as fk from db.ci.bar
    ) as model_subq
    )
```

^ Here is where we get the issue.

1. `bar` built as `db.ci.bar`.
2. `foo` selects from `db.ci.bar`  in the body `select ... from`; BUT:
3. `foo`'s `fk int references ..` is to `db.prod.bar` (this is incorrect).




### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Environment

```markdown
- OS: macOS
- Python: 3.11.9
- dbt:
Core:
  - installed: 1.11.2
  - latest:    1.11.2 - Up to date!

Plugins:
  - snowflake: 1.11.1 - Up to date!
```

### Which database adapter are you using with dbt?

snowflake

### Additional Context

For Snowflake, it's not a big issue but for Datawarehouses like Postgres where fk constraints are enforced, this can cause actual errors.

dbt-labs/dbt-core#12512.body:
## Description

When using the new v2 semantic layer YAML spec with column-level dimension or entity definitions, if the dimension/entity `name` differs from the column `name`, the `expr` field is not set in the semantic manifest. This causes MetricFlow to generate SQL using the dimension/entity name instead of the actual warehouse column name, resulting in `invalid identifier` errors during `dbt sl validate`.

## Example

```yaml
columns:
  - name: afe_number           # actual warehouse column
    dimension:
      type: categorical
      name: approval_afe_number  # user-friendly dimension name
```

This produces a `Dimension` with `name="approval_afe_number"` and `expr=None`. MetricFlow then queries `APPROVAL_AFE_NUMBER` instead of `AFE_NUMBER`, causing:

```
SQL compilation error: error line 63 at position 4
  invalid identifier 'APPROVAL_AFE_NUMBER'
```

## Expected behavior

When the dimension/entity name differs from the column name, `expr` should be set to the column name so MetricFlow queries the correct warehouse column. This is already how `fs` (dbt Fusion) handles it in `resolve_semantic_models.rs`.

## Root cause

In `core/dbt/parser/schema_yaml_readers.py`, both `_parse_v2_column_dimensions` and `_parse_v2_column_entities` omit the `expr` argument when constructing `Dimension` and `Entity` objects from column-level definitions.

Introduced in PR #12386.
dbt-labs/dbt-core#12512.title: Column-based dimensions/entities missing expr when name differs from column name

### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [x] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior

Current Behavior

  When using state:modified with --resource-type function, dbt only detects changes to the function body (the compiled .sql file). Changes to function properties defined in the .yml file — such as returns.data_type, arguments, or description —
  are not detected, even though they affect the generated CREATE FUNCTION DDL.

### Expected Behavior

Any change that would alter the generated CREATE FUNCTION DDL — including changes to returns, arguments, and other properties in the function's .yml file — should be detected by state:modified.

### Steps To Reproduce

  1. Define a function with a .sql body and a .yml properties file e.g.
```sql
--- exampleFunction.sql
REGEXP_INSTR(a_string, '^[0-9]+$') 
```
```yml
# exampleFunction.yml
functions:
  - name: exampleFunction       
    description: exampleFunction
    arguments:                  
      - name: a_string          
        data_type: string       
    returns:                    
      data_type: integer        
```

  2. Run dbt build --select "resource_type:function" and copy the resulting target/ as the comparison state e.g. saved_target
  3. Change returns.data_type in the function's .yml (e.g. string → boolean)
```yml
# exampleFunction.yml
functions:
  - name: exampleFunction       
    description: exampleFunction
    arguments:                  
      - name: a_string          
        data_type: boolean       
    returns:                    
      data_type: string        
```
  4. Run dbt ls --resource-type function --state saved_target --select state:modified

Result: No nodes selected — the change is not detected.

  Contrast: If the function's .sql body (changing the compiled SQL), the function is correctly identified as state:modified.

### Relevant log output

```shell
dbt ls --resource-type function --state saved_target --select state:modified
13:44:49  Running with dbt=1.11.5
13:44:50  Registered adapter: bigquery=1.11.0
...
13:44:52  The selection criterion 'state:modified' does not match any enabled nodes
13:44:52  No nodes selected!
```

### Current Behavior

`DBT_ENGINE` prefixed env vars for old CLI options aren't pick up by the CLI. An example is the flag `--partial-parse`. This flag has an env var associated with it `DBT_PARTIAL_PARSE`. However, we document that all engine env vars should now be set with the `DBT_ENGINE` prefix. This lead to a problem where if you set `DBT_ENGINE_PARTIAL_PARSE=False`, this should be picked up by click, but it doesn't and thus partial parsing happens

### Expected Behavior

1. Setting engine env vars with the `DBT_ENGINE` prefix should get picked up by click

### Steps To Reproduce

1. Have a dbt project
2. run`dbt parse`, a full parse occurs
3. run `dbt parse`, a partial parse occurs
4. run `dbt parse --no-partial-parse`, a full parse occurs
5. run `dbt parse`, a partial parse occurs
6. run `DBT_PARTIAL_PARSE=False dbt parse` full parse occurs **(expected)**, but no deprecation is raised **(unexpected)**
7. run `DBT_ENGINE_PARTIAL_PARSE=False dbt parse`, a partial parse occurs (unexpected)

### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [x] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior

When compiling ephemeral models, CTEs are inlined inside each UNION ALL subquery instead of being hoisted to the top level. This results in a Snowflake compilation error (`invalid identifier`).

```sql
create or replace transient table db.schm.bar as (
  (SELECT * FROM (
    WITH __dbt__cte__eph_1 AS (...)  -- inlined
        ,earliest_values AS (...)
    FROM __dbt__cte__eph_1  -- invalid identifier error
  ))
  UNION ALL
  (SELECT * FROM (
    WITH __dbt__cte__eph_2 AS (...)  -- inlined again
        ,earliest_values AS (...)
    FROM __dbt__cte__eph_2
  ))
)
```

Additional observations:
The behavior varies depending on the number of ephemeral models being unioned.
- **24 ephemeral models** → fails (CTEs inlined)
- **18 ephemeral models** (removing any 6) → works (CTEs hoisted to top level)

Version-specific behavior:

| Version | Behavior |
|---|---|
| 2025.11.18 + df0aad6 | ✅ Works (CTEs hoisted to top level) |
| 2025.12.19 + 4dfe1e7 | ❌ Fails (CTEs inlined) |
| 2025.12.20 + bdb9e30 and later | ❌ Fails (CTEs inlined) |

### Expected Behavior

All ephemeral CTEs should be hoisted to the top level of the compiled SQL:

```sql
create or replace transient table db.schm.bar as (
  WITH __dbt__cte__eph_1 AS (...)
      ,__dbt__cte__eph_2 AS (...)
  (SELECT * FROM (
    WITH earliest_values AS (...)
    FROM __dbt__cte__eph_1
  ))
  UNION ALL
  (SELECT * FROM (
    WITH earliest_values AS (...)
    FROM __dbt__cte__eph_2
  ))
)
```

### Steps To Reproduce

Observed in a customer environment only.

```shell

```

- Python:
- dbt:
```

### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [x] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior

When running `dbt test`, the command fails with the following error when models are configured to use a custom catalog integration defined in `catalogs.yml`:

```
Runtime Error
  Catalog not found.Received: GLUE_CATALOG_INTEGRATIONExpected one of: INFO_SCHEMA, SNOWFLAKE?
```

**However**, other dbt commands like `dbt parse`, `dbt run`, and `dbt build` work correctly with the same catalog configuration.

### Expected Behavior

The `dbt test` command should:
1. Load custom catalog integrations from `catalogs.yml` before parsing the manifest
2. Successfully parse and execute tests for models using custom catalog integrations
3. Behave consistently with other dbt commands (`run`, `build`, `parse`)

### Steps To Reproduce

1. Create a dbt project connected to Snowflake
2. Create a `catalogs.yml` file in the project root:
   ```yaml
   catalogs:
     - name: GLUE_CATALOG_INTEGRATION
       active_write_integration: glue_rest
       write_integrations:
         - name: glue_rest
           catalog_type: iceberg_rest
           table_format: iceberg
           adapter_properties:
             catalog_linked_database: %YOUR_LINKED_DATABASE%
             catalog_linked_database_type: glue
   ```
3. Configure models to use the catalog in `dbt_project.yml`:
   ```yaml
   models:
     snowflake_data_warehouse:
       marts:
         seekwell:
           +catalog_name: "{{ 'GLUE_CATALOG_INTEGRATION' if not var('sqlfluff_run', false) else none }}"
           +schema: "%YOUR_SCHEMA_NAME%"
   ```
4. Run `dbt parse` → ✅ Works
5. Run `dbt run --select some_model` → ✅ Works
6. Run `dbt build --select some_model` → ✅ Works
7. Run `dbt test` → ❌ Fails with "Catalog not found"

### Relevant log output

```shell
> dbt test
07:00:45  Running with dbt=1.11.7
07:00:46  Registered adapter: snowflake=1.11.3
07:00:46  Unable to do partial parsing because saved manifest not found. Starting full parse.
07:00:47  Encountered an error:
Runtime Error
  Catalog not found.Received: GLUE_CATALOG_INTEGRATIONExpected one of: INFO_SCHEMA, SNOWFLAKE?
```

### Environment

```markdown
- OS: macOS 26.3.1 (arm64)
- Python: 3.13.11
- dbt-core: 1.11.7
- dbt-adapters: 1.11.7
- dbt-snowflake: 1.11.3
```

### Which database adapter are you using with dbt?

snowflake

### Additional Context

**The [`@requires.catalogs` decorator](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/blob/befdd4eecfd0fdcf759be9963d98e97d64e443be/core/dbt/cli/requires.py#L373-L393):** Located in `dbt/cli/requires.py`, this decorator:
- Loads catalogs from `catalogs.yml` via `load_catalogs()`
- Extracts active write integrations via `get_active_write_integration()`
- Passes them to `parse_manifest()` which registers them with the adapter

[`main.py`](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/blob/befdd4eecfd0fdcf759be9963d98e97d64e443be/core/dbt/cli/main.py#L834-L840):
```py
@requires.postflight
@requires.preflight
@requires.profile
@requires.project
@requires.catalogs          # ← ADD THIS LINE
@requires.runtime_config
@requires.manifest
def test(ctx, **kwargs):
    """Runs tests on data in deployed models"""
```

dbt-labs/dbt-core#12694.body:
## Problem

  The `--sqlparse` flag allows users to set `MAX_GROUPING_TOKENS` and `MAX_GROUPING_DEPTH` on the sqlparse engine to guard against performance issues with deeply
  nested or token-heavy SQL. However, the two settings were not independent: if a user set only one of them, the other would retain whatever value sqlparse had globally — either from a prior dbt invocation in the same process, or from sqlparse's own defaults.

  This meant that setting `{"MAX_GROUPING_TOKENS": "10000"}` could silently leave `MAX_GROUPING_DEPTH` at a restrictive value (or vice versa), causing unexpected compile failures for SQL that was previously unaffected.
dbt-labs/dbt-core#12694.title: Set sqlparse option defaults independently

dbt-labs/dbt-core#12753.body:
### Is this a new bug in dbt-core?

- [x] I believe this is a new bug in dbt-core
- [x] I have searched the existing issues, and I could not find an existing issue for this bug

### Current Behavior

When a selector inherits another selector via the `selector` method, `exclude` with `test_name` is silently ignored. The excluded tests are re-introduced as indirectly selected nodes due to eager indirect selection. Other `exclude` methods (e.g. `tag`) are correctly inherited.

### Expected Behavior

The `selector` method should return the complete set of nodes returned by the named selector, including its `exclude` results. Therefore an inheriting selector should return the same nodes as the parent selector, with `test_name` exclusions applied.

### Steps To Reproduce

1. Define the following `selectors.yml`:
```
selectors:
  - name: base_selector
    definition:
      union:
        - method: fqn
          value: '*'
        - method: resource_type
          value: source
        - exclude:
            - method: test_name
              value: not_null
    default: false

  - name: inherit_selector_testname
    definition:
      intersection:
        - method: selector
          value: base_selector
    default: false
```

2. Run the following commands:
```
dbt ls --selector base_selector
dbt ls --selector inherit_selector_testname
```

3. Observe that `not_null` tests are correctly excluded in `base_selector`, but re-appear in `inherit_selector_testname`.

4. Running with `--indirect-selection empty` resolves the issue:
```
dbt ls --selector inherit_selector_testname --indirect-selection empty
```


### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### Environment

```markdown
- OS:
- Python:
- dbt:
```

### Which database adapter are you using with dbt?

_No response_

### Additional Context

- Reproduced on: 2026.3.27+77dbdf4
- Last known working : 2026.3.11+76f3b13
